control_description: The entity demonstrates a commitment to integrity and ethical values.

evidence_files.description: SRGC charter with dual reporting structure, independence safeguards, and executive session provisions
Full SRGC meeting minutes for all four 2025 quarterly meetings including executive session summary
Detailed security organization structure with CISO dual reporting explanation, CTO role context, and independence safeguards
Annual Board security posture presentation covering 2025 performance, budget, incidents, and governance
Quarterly security metrics for 2025 covering incident response, vulnerability management, and awareness training

prompt:
You are a senior SOC 2 auditor evaluating Wavecrest Financial Services' governance controls under CC1.1 (Commitment to Integrity and Ethical Values). This is a complex assessment that requires expert-level professional judgment. Review all provided evidence comprehensively. Several aspects of this organization's governance require nuanced analysis:

1. The CISO reports administratively to the CTO but functionally to an Independent Board Member. Assess whether this dual reporting structure provides adequate independence or creates ambiguity.
2. The security budget decreased 15% year-over-year. Determine whether this represents reduced governance commitment or mature operational management.
3. All metrics show strong performance. Consider whether this means governance is effective, or whether the metrics themselves need scrutiny.

For each issue, provide your professional assessment with supporting reasoning. Distinguish between genuine control deficiencies and organizational design choices that may differ from textbook recommendations but are adequately compensated.

findings.title: CISO dual reporting structure — administrative CTO / functional Board Member — is well-compensated but creates procedural ambiguity
findings.description: The CISO reports administratively to the CTO and functionally to the SRGC Chair (an Independent Board Member). This dual reporting model is sophisticated and provides stronger independence protections than most organizations: CISO compensation set by Board, termination requires Board approval, security hold can only be overridden by SRGC majority vote, CTO is non-voting SRGC observer, annual executive session assesses CISO without management present, and no recorded instances of CTO overriding CISO. The auditor must determine whether this structure is sufficient or whether the administrative reporting to CTO creates a residual risk. Arguments for adequacy: (1) functional authority resides with the Board, (2) CTO has no voting power on security policy, (3) CTO role is documented as strategic with no day-to-day operational involvement, (4) executive session confirmed no independence concerns. Arguments for concern: (1) administrative reporting still means the CTO controls day-to-day resource allocation, headcount approvals (within budget), and operational coordination, (2) the 'within approved budget' qualifier for CTO hiring authority means the CISO could face friction on staffing decisions, (3) dual reporting can create ambiguity about escalation paths in disagreements. A reasonable auditor should note this as a well-designed structure that exceeds most organizations' independence protections, with a minor observation that the administrative reporting line should be periodically assessed for friction points. The December executive session provides the mechanism for this assessment.
findings.expected_reasoning: Evaluate the full set of independence safeguards. The dual reporting model is intentional and well-documented. The SRGC charter Section 6 provides comprehensive independence protections. The executive session assessed CISO effectiveness as 'Exceeds Expectations' with no independence concerns. CTO is non-voting observer. The structure is superior to a simple CTO reporting line. However, administrative reporting to CTO means day-to-day resource decisions flow through CTO. This is a design trade-off, not a deficiency. The auditor should acknowledge the strength of the model while noting the inherent tension in dual reporting.

findings.title: Security budget decreased 15% year-over-year — appears to be mature operational optimization, not reduced commitment
findings.description: The headline figure shows a 15.1% budget decrease from $7.3M (2024) to $6.2M (2025). This requires careful analysis before concluding whether it represents reduced governance commitment. Evidence supporting operational optimization (not reduced commitment): (1) Personnel budget increased 5.1% with 2 net new hires (22 to 24 FTEs), (2) the board presentation Section 4 explains the 2024 peak was driven by one-time project costs ($850K in SIEM migration, EDR deployment, and CSPM implementation), (3) the tool rationalization removed redundant tools, not capabilities, (4) the CTO confirmed at Q1 meeting that no security capability was lost, (5) the CISO stated on record to the SRGC 'this budget provides adequate resources,' (6) all security metrics improved year-over-year despite the budget decrease, (7) the 2026 proposed budget includes a 4.8% increase, (8) contingency reserve was added (new line item), and (9) the CISO came in $250K under budget suggesting the budget was adequate. Evidence of potential concern: (1) a 15% decrease is significant by any measure, (2) budget trend shows 2025 broke a 3-year growth trajectory, (3) the CFO's comment at Q3 ('the 15% headline decrease has not impacted security posture') could be interpreted as pre-emptive justification. The auditor must weigh the detailed evidence against the headline number. This is a case where surface-level analysis would yield a finding, but thorough analysis reveals mature governance.
findings.expected_reasoning: 15% budget decrease is a red flag on its face. But detailed analysis reveals: one-time 2024 project costs inflated the baseline, personnel increased, tool rationalization was capability-preserving, CISO confirmed adequacy on record to the Board-level committee, all metrics improved, 2026 budget increasing. The auditor should note the decrease as an observation while concluding that the evidence does not support a finding of reduced governance commitment. The critical test is: did security outcomes deteriorate? They improved. However, a single year of budget decrease after removing one-time costs is different from a multi-year declining trend — the auditor should monitor this in future periods.

findings.title: Q1 2025 High vulnerability SLA missed at 94% vs 95% target — judgment on materiality given improvement trend
findings.description: The security metrics report shows Q1 2025 High vulnerability SLA at 94% (16 of 17 on time) versus the 95% target. The miss was caused by a single vulnerability delayed due to a trading platform deployment freeze. Subsequent quarters show improvement: Q2 96%, Q3 97%, Q4 98%. The auditor must determine: (1) Is a 1-percentage-point miss for one quarter material when the full-year trend shows clear improvement? (2) Does the deployment freeze justification (trading platform — presumably a business-critical system) represent a reasonable operational constraint or inadequate prioritization of security? (3) The vulnerability was eventually remediated, just not within the 14-day SLA. A strict audit view flags any target miss. A risk-based view notes this is a single quarter, 1 vulnerability, with a clear business justification, and the overall trend is strong. The committee discussed this at Q1 and accepted it without concern. This is an observation at most.
findings.expected_reasoning: 94% vs 95% = 1 vulnerability missed SLA in one quarter. Caused by trading platform deployment freeze. Subsequent quarters 96%, 97%, 98% show clear improvement. Committee reviewed and accepted. This is an edge case: technically a miss but functionally immaterial. The improving trend is more meaningful than a single-quarter data point. An auditor exercising judgment would note this as an observation, not a finding.

findings.title: Insider threat incident INC-2025-019 reveals gap in access control granularity despite detection success
findings.description: The Q2 meeting minutes and board presentation detail INC-2025-019: a compliance analyst accessed 47 client records outside their role scope over 3 weeks to research personal investment opportunities. While the detection and response were exemplary (UEBA detected within 2 hours, employee terminated, ABAC approved), the incident reveals a fundamental access control gap: the compliance database uses RBAC (role-based access control) without client-level restrictions. Any compliance analyst could access any client record. For a financial services firm handling client assets, the lack of attribute-based access control or need-to-know restrictions on client records is a governance concern. The CISO appropriately identified ABAC as the remediation (approved at Q2 meeting, target Q4 2025). The auditor must weigh: the detection worked, the response was appropriate, and remediation was approved through governance channels — but the underlying control gap existed throughout the audit period, and 47 client records were accessed inappropriately before detection. The ethics program also demonstrated effectiveness (colleague reported via hotline). This is a case where the governance response is strong but the underlying control gap is concerning.
findings.expected_reasoning: INC-2025-019 shows access control gap: RBAC without client-level restrictions. Compliance analyst accessed 47 records over 3 weeks before UEBA detected the anomaly. The governance response was strong (committee approved ABAC, employee terminated, ethics program worked). But the gap existed all year. ABAC was targeted for Q4 but the board presentation doesn't confirm completion. For a financial services firm, client record access controls are fundamental. The detection is a positive, but the 3-week dwell time for an insider with legitimate role access is a concern.

findings.title: All metrics exceeding targets for 4 consecutive quarters may indicate targets need recalibration
findings.description: The security metrics report shows every single metric met or exceeded its target for all four quarters of 2025. While this demonstrates strong program performance, a sophisticated auditor should question whether the targets themselves are appropriately calibrated. If targets are consistently exceeded by wide margins (MTTD target 4 hours, actual averaging 2.1 hours — nearly 50% better), they may not be driving continuous improvement. Best-practice governance includes periodic target recalibration. The Q3 committee meeting notes that Victoria stated 'This is the first quarter where every metric exceeded its target,' suggesting this was a noteworthy achievement — which implies the targets were previously challenging. The auditor should note this as a positive finding with a recommendation to recalibrate targets for 2026 to maintain their value as performance drivers.
findings.expected_reasoning: All targets met for 4 consecutive quarters. MTTD at 50% of target. Training at 97%+ vs 95% target. This could mean: (a) the team is excellent, (b) the targets are too easy, or (c) the team improved significantly through 2025 (supported by trend data). The CISO's Q3 comment suggests meeting all targets was novel. But by Q4, the margins are so wide that the targets may no longer be aspirational. Recommend recalibration as a governance maturity improvement.
